title: Dorset apple cake
subtitle: Rustic British apple cake with a demerara crust
excerpt: A deep, moist apple cake from the West Country: chunks of apple folded into a butter sponge, spiced with mixed spice, finished with a crunchy demerara crust. Made in a round tin, cut in wedges. Keeps for four days and is better the day after baking.
sourceNotes: The Dorset apple cake is a documented regional British bake, with recipes appearing in West Country household cooking manuals from the nineteenth century onward. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): documents the Dorset apple cake as a regional speciality, noting the use of cooking or eating apples depending on the season and the importance of a generous layer of coarse sugar on top. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: fruit cakes with chopped raw apple mixed into a butter sponge are documented in the chapter on plain cakes.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap slices in clingfilm and freeze. Defrost at room temperature for 1 hour.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake improves on day two as the apple moisture distributes through the crumb. Store wrapped at room temperature.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. The apple releases moisture as it bakes — check with a skewer at 45 minutes; the centre may need the full 50

glossaryTerms.definition: A crunchy surface layer formed by scattering demerara sugar over the top of a cake batter before baking. The large, coarse crystals do not fully dissolve during baking, leaving a slightly gritty, caramelised crust. On a fruit cake the contrast between the crunchy top and the moist crumb below is the defining textural feature.

content.text: Dorset apple cake is an unpretentious cake: chunks of apple in a butter sponge, lightly spiced, with a crunchy demerara top. It does not look dramatic but it keeps well and tastes better the day after baking, when the apple moisture has had time to work through the crumb. Use eating apples for a milder cake or Bramleys for a sharper one.

items.prepNote: about 2–3 medium, peeled, cored, cut into 1 cm pieces

content.text: Preheat the oven to 175°C fan. Butter and line the tin. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} until pale and fluffy. Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time. Fold in the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{mixed-spice}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}, then stir in the {{whole-milk}}. Fold in the {{apple-eating}} pieces.
Spoon the batter into the tin and level the surface. Scatter the {{demerara-sugar}} evenly over the top. This is the

: the crystals will caramelise and stay slightly crunchy after baking. Bake for 45–50 minutes until a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean and the top feels firm when pressed. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes before turning out. Serve in wedges.
